sentences.text: Yes.
So that-- this-- this was my feeling.
My instinct was to get out.
My mother, on the other hand, didn't believe that my sister was dead, so she felt that there is-- my sister is there somewhere.
And, of course, we didn't know that my father was dead.
So she said, you know, my father is there, so she didn't want to go.
This sounds as if one had a choice.
Of course, one didn't.
But this was a more or less a theoretical thing, that I kept saying we've got to, we've got to.
And she kept saying, I'm not going anywhere.
I'm staying here, because-- because Zsuzsa and-- and my father is there.

So the way the-- the system worked, when there was a selection, a big selection, they-- they had a cordon, a line, a human cordon made, so that people couldn't hide beyond-- you couldn't go beyond that cordon.
Because, obviously, everyone tried to hide, because everyone was frightened of-- of straight into the gas chamber, with good reason.
So my mother, because she had a position, because she was the camp doctor, didn't have to-- didn't have to appear at the selection.
She was exempt.
In fact, I rather think that she wasn't allowed-- I think.
Now, the cordon was made up by people who had some sort of a job, whether that was-- whatever-- whatever.
People who were bringing the food in-- that was a job.
Or people who-- oh, I don't know-- whatever.
But they had some sort of standing, and they made up the-- the cordon.
Well, of course, my sister and I had nothing like that.
And my mother-- came a selection.
And my mother insisted that we, my sister and I, stand in that line so that we don't-- excuse me-- get into the-- into the selection.
Because I know-- I can understand.
She didn't want us to be separated from her again.
Yet she wanted to stay, so we had to stay.
And I do so clearly remember saying to her, we can't do that.
We cannot get into that line, because they know how many officials are good to be in that line.
You can't do it.
But my mother absolutely insisted and pushed us into that line.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We were-- I can't tell you how frightened we were, my sister and I.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came the German, naturally with gun in hand, and looked at the line, and said, there are more people here.
Nothing was said.
Said, all right.
And he had his gun, and went [POINTING] one, two, three, four, five, six, seven. 12 instead of 10, he said.
The two people who don't belong, step out.
Well, we didn't.
Because we knew that if that happens, that's it.
So we just kept quiet.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So the German said, the two people step out, or the line is dead.
Well, obviously-- well, I don't know what my sister was thinking, because we couldn't talk to each other.
But certainly, I'm sure that she was thinking the same thing.
We've got to step out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here is the miracle-- our miracle, somebody else's tragedy.
That very moment, there was this incredible screaming from under the barrack, the barrack that was next to us.
They spotted a young woman who was hiding under the barracks.
And they started to drag her out.
And she, of course, resisted and screamed.
And the German with the gun in his hands turned around and ran up to the barrack so that-- to give a hand against this horrible, strong, Jewish woman.
And at that moment, my sister and I, we ran.
We just broke the line and ran.
And we ran into-- among the-- the-- the whole lot of people who were lined up for the search.
We threw our clothes off, because the others were already-- already naked.
And in the meantime, this girl was beaten to death.
She died right there.
That would have been us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So my mother saw all this happening, of course.
And she also-- she ran into that line.
She got herself into the line where she wasn't supposed to be, and she threw everything off as well.
And so we went for the selection.
And we got out.
We were taken to Belsen.
Well, for that to happen-- if I were reading that somewhere or if I saw it on the movies, I'd say don't give me that.
But that's how it happened.
And I think that it's miracles like that-- miracles like that had to happen to most people to survive.
Because the chances of nothing like that happening was nil.
You-- something had to constantly happen for you to survive.
Well, this is what happened to us.
So we were put on a train and got to Belsen.
When we arrived in Belsen, things weren't nearly as bad.
We were just about the first ones to arrive there.
And in comparison to Auschwitz, at that stage, it was far, far better-- at that stage.
The-- we were, of course we were in a-- on the-- on the ground or some sort of a building on the ground.
But it was better.
At least we were on the ground and not pressed in.
And the food was actually far, far, far superior to what we got in Auschwitz, because Auschwitz, you didn't have food.
You had garbage-- literally-- pieces of wood in a soup.
It wasn't food.
But then, there, it was.
It was food.
So it was better.
But when I--
Oh, well, I remember it was always something liquidy.
But they were nice sort of soups and nice sort of vegetable concoction.
You could actually eat it.
So it wasn't-- we didn't get any meat, or we didn't-- you know, you didn't get meat and potatoes-- of course not-- but some sort of edible concoction.
And funny enough, I remember the taste of one of them.
And it was rather as if it had-- had peas in it.
Whether it did or not, I can't say.
But that was the one-- that was mashed peas.
So it was-- it was edible.
We got lunch, something for lunch.
Probably for lunch, I would think that we would have got just bread and a piece of cheese or something.
And at night, we had this concoction.
But it was at least something that you could-- you could swallow without wanting to be ill.
Because in Auschwitz, anything that you put in your mouth, you wanted to be ill.
And you only ate because one knew that if you don't, that's the end of you.
So-- Now, in Belsen, things, of course, got-- when I say quickly, that's-- that's understating it.
Within virtually two weeks, things were back to Auschwitz standard.
Because some of the guards were brought down from Auschwitz.
There were a lot of Hungarian guards.
I-- I actually can't remember talking to one, but I knew that there are a lot of Hungarian guards there, and they were, of course, terrible.
Would have been September that we arrived.
And we weren't there very long-- only, I'd say, about five weeks.
And my memory of Belsen, as I said, at the beginning, as as as as a sort of place where-- after Auschwitz, it was all right to start with.
But then within days-- because more people came, and the guards from Auschwitz were sent down, so professionalism took over.
And then it was-- apart from the fact that, at that stage, there was no gas chamber, but tortures, whippings, no-- no difference whatsoever.
I do remember one scene in particular, actually, where we were in the center of some sort of-- of-- well, in the open.
And we were all gathered in the center.
And two women, [GERMAN], were walking around with whips and just whipping people.
Whoever was on the outside was whipped.
So you can imagine the-- the terrible scene of everyone trying to get inside-- within-- inside the crowd.
So that stands out in my memory.
And the other thing that stands out are the death carts.
I remember the death carts-- you know, the carts that picked up the dead.
Can you describe them for us?
Well, yes.
They were like trolleys-- big trolleys.
And people were pushing them.
And-- and the way the dead were thrown-- mind you, some were not dead.
Because they'd go into the hospital, sick room, and if someone looked dead enough, it was just picked up.

And the way they were thrown onto this cart, two people-- one taking the hands, and the other-- the other two legs, and go And these bodies, or near-dead bodies, were just flung onto these-- onto these carts.
And can you imagine someone being alive?
And then, seeing it-- there was no gas chamber, as I said.
They'd burn them, bury them.
And the fact that there was someone alive, well, it's their bad luck.
So they would have burned people who were still alive.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So that-- that I remember very clearly.
The-- some of these scenes, the films that one sees-- not film films, not movie films, but documentaries.
I'm sure that you've seen these-- these-- when the Germans were made to do it, after the liberation-- well, it was exactly the same except Jews with doing it, and some of them were alive.
When I say Jews were doing it, they-- those who were ordered.
This is not that they were doing it for fun.
But they were ordered.
That was their job.
And-- and some of them were alive.
So that was-- that-- we were there for, as I said, about five weeks, I'd say.
And then we got into a selection.
And there it was no-- there was no more-- no more argument or, you know, whatever with my mother.
We got in-- we were taken into selection and taken out, and we were taken to Duderstadt, which is a very small place in-- it's about 30 kilometers from Gottingen in West Germany.
And by the time we got there, we could hear the-- the English guns, English and American guns, [NON-ENGLISH].
So we knew that somehow, somehow, we are getting there.
We didn't know whether we'd survive.
Well, we were sure we would.
We always were.
But anyway, this was a very small work camp.
It was a labor camp.
It wasn't a concentration camp.
And it certainly wasn't an extermination camp, either.
It was simply a little working camp.
Now, there, truly, the situation was far, far better, because there were little houses, actual houses, built by-- brick houses.
And they had rooms.
And in the rooms, yeah, they were bunks, but decent-- decent beds-- two beds, like kids have.
So the situation there, from that point-- and we even had some heating.
There was a little heater there that we-- because now, we're getting into winter.
This was November.
And we-- we worked in a munition factory.
And my sister and I-- beg your pardon-- my sister and my mother were in one shift, and I was by myself in another.
They were 12-hour shifts.
So I-- I saw nothing of them.
Because we just didn't-- except on a Sunday.
We worked seven days, 12 hours-- beg your pardon-- six days, 12 hours, but not on Sunday.
And-- and I remember my birthday, November 30, we were there.
And my work transport was walking up into the factory, and theirs was coming back from their shift.
And my mother said, happy birthday.
I remember that.
And, so, the situation was really and truly acceptable there.
If that had been right through, would have been all right.
There is one thing that I-- I'd like to-- I'd like to tell the story of-- no, two things, in Duderstadt.
One was that there was-- the German commander was living-- well, as I said, they were brick houses.
And one of the rather attractive, nice, big, big house was his, the commander.
And he had a girlfriend, a beautiful young woman, [GERMAN], and of course, called Marguerite.
And they had a love affair, a big love affair.
And needless to say, Jews were not allowed near that building, of course.
And who'd want to go there, anyway?
But we had, in my block, there was this unfortunate woman who was-- [POINTING] I think, she was already gone.
And something happened to her.
I'm not quite sure what it was, but something that she didn't like one night.
And she marched up to the commandant's house and banged the door and went in.
And there was the commander with Marguerite in bed.
So you can imagine the welcome.
So the German, I think, literally kicked-- literally kicked her out.
She flew out and-- and landed on the ground.
And she was picked up by another German, probably SS.
And because of what she's done, she was beaten up.
So she went back to the commandant to complain that she was beaten up.
And he killed her.
Beat her to death.
She was beaten to the extent where she died.
So that-- the other thing that I want to tell you about-- about Duderstadt was that a woman in my-- in the room that I was, was pregnant.
Of course, this is-- this was still well-- with the Hungarians, she got pregnant before she was taken.
Because this is only still November.
So there was plenty-- only six months in camp at that stage.
So she was-- what I'm trying to say is she was not pregnant to a German, but pregnant.
How she survived being pregnant, I have no idea.
That-- that was unbelievable.
But she did.
But Germans didn't notice, whatever, but she did.
And she gave birth in Duderstadt.
And the child, when she was born, was-- was very, very badly incapacitated.
It had no back passage.
Interestingly, there was another case like that in Auschwitz where no back passage, so there must have been something, somehow-- well, I mustn't say that, because I don't know.
But it's interesting that two-- two babies born with the same affliction.
Anyway, this baby was born, no back passage, so it was quite obvious that that child had to die within 24 hours.
So she-- she was-- the mother was begging everyone not to tell the Germans.
But, of course, that was-- that was crazy.
Not that-- I don't think anyone told the Germans, but the child was crying.
And the Germans, of course, knew, and came to get the child.
And this woman fell on her knees and begged the Germans.
It was a scene out of a movie-- on her knees, holding on to the feet of the-- not to take the baby, but of course they did.
And she went blind.
From that day on, she was blind.
But she was-- again, by a miracle, she survived.
She was-- everyone, of course, was helping her, so the Germans didn't realize that she's blind.
So she survived.
We were in Duderstadt till April.
So that was quite a long stay there.
And-- and I got very ill, very sick.
And they put me into hospital there.
And then when I got better, they put me into the kitchen to work.
When I say 'they,' that's the Jewish head-- the Jewish head of the camp, who happened to be from my hometown.
And she-- she-- she said, you're going to-- you're going to go into the kitchen.
So from then on, I was well fed.
In actual fact, the food there really wasn't bad.
And everyone was-- and everyone had quite decent-- were decently fed.
But we were even better fed, because we could eat all the vegetables.
And we were-- actually, it was interesting-- accused by the Jews that we would be eating their food.
But of course, that wasn't true, because we were given buckets.
And the bucket had to be filled.
Whether we put something in our mouth or not in between didn't matter, because the bucket had to be filled.
But we were accused of-- of eating their food.
But that's understandable, after all, in that atmosphere-- anything.
But that's just by the way.
Yes.
Magda.
Something with a V.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Not [?
Vertisch ?], but something terribly, terribly similar.
Vermesch? ?] [?
Vernedt? ?] Something like that.
Actually, she never came back, because she was the girlfriend of one of the-- one of the SS.
And I didn't know that she did anything, myself.
She was very good to me.
But there were some complaints.
And she never, ever showed her face in [INAUDIBLE] again.
But she was good to me.
I can't-- and I don't even know what she was accused of.
But she was accused of-- but I guess the very fact of being a German's girlfriend is bad enough.
But apparently, instead of helping, there was some-- but I can't say.
I truly don't know what she did.
I'm thankful to her because she helped me.
So the-- the front was coming nearer.
We could hear it every day, and louder, and louder, and louder.
And then we thought, we'll be free soon.
Instead of which, of course, they packed us up, put into another cattle truck, and across Germany.
Well, it was a rather interesting journey, in that we really saw Germany collapse.
Because this was April.
And we truly did.
We saw-- we went through-- one of the places I remember very clearly was Magdeburg.
And there was nothing.
There was absolutely nothing.
There were some walls standing, but behind the walls, there was nothing.
And, you know, every time we saw one of these buildings, how wonderful, how wonderful.
Every time we heard a siren, we said, wonderful, wonderful.
Naturally, did this.
And as we were taken through Germany, we saw soldiers without-- without proper clothing.
We saw them literary running the-- away from the Russians.
Well, the Russians were still pretty far, but all the same, they were all going up west as  far as they could.
And we saw civilians in incredible-- By the way, I must say-- I must tell you that people are saying that the Germans didn't know.

In Belsen, the camp was terribly near the little town, so near that from the camp, from a particular corner, you could actually see a bridge, which was just a little bridge in-- in-- in the little-- little town.
And civilians, when we went-- when they-- when they took us for a shower, which wasn't often, the shower area was sort of near the end of the camp.
We saw civilians walking outside.
And they saw us.
They saw us.
No.
Only in Duderstadt we had a few-- a few nasty-- but no.
No, they didn't.
But they saw us.
Belsen was part of that little town.
And they're saying they didn't know.
[LAUGHS] It's so ridiculous.
It is so bizarre that anyone can argue and believe that they didn't know.
Anyway, that's just by the way.
I just remembered that.
But in Duderstadt, yes, we did see a lot of civilians, of course, because that was, again, in Little Duderstadt.
And many of them just didn't look at us.
Many of them, stinking Jews, these Jews, or that Jews.
But they didn't touch us.
They didn't.
But one of the [GERMAN], she must have been crazy, in Duderstadt.
She-- one thing she loved doing-- there was this-- this was in the kitchen-- a huge oven, with, of course, fire.
And any mouse or rat she found, she put it in there alive.
And we had to watch those poor little animals being put in there alive.
And you know what she said to us?
Well, actually, she said it to me.
Said, tell me-- actually, why are you here?
And, well, I said, because we're Jewish.
Oh, that.
You must have done something.
Are you a street-walker?
I said, no.
No, I'm not a street-walker.
I'm just Jewish.
She didn't want to believe it.
[POINTING] She must have been touched in the head.
She didn't know why we were there.
Would you believe that?
And she was one of the--
[GERMAN] .
Yeah, she was one of the [GERMAN].
The German SS [GERMAN].
And she didn't know why we were there.
Yes.
She did.
She did.
But I think she was also-- she was cuckoo.
You'd have to be to put live animals into the-- all right.
She doesn't want to have rats around.
OK.
Put up-- do whatever.
But put it in the fire?
Put it in the oven?
So I'm sure that she was cuckoo.
But she didn't know why we were there.
No, I believed her.
Truly I did.
She wasn't particularly cruel.
She wasn't nice, but she wasn't particularly cruel.
She was-- she was just, I think, a crazy lady, who happened to-- who happened to be sent on a-- on a-- on this sort of duty.
So then-- now, this is still Duderstadt.
This is still Duderstadt, yeah.
So, as I said, we were taken out.
And-- and on the way-- and we went-- that's what I was saying-- we actually saw the Germans running.
We did.
You know, because they were-- there was panic.
Because the-- from the west, of course, the Americans were coming.
And from the east, the Russians were coming.
And they were just running like rats everywhere.
And we loved that.
We really did.

Anyway, we were taken-- it took a long time to get to Theresienstadt, because from West Germany, under those circumstances, with the-- with the rail-- with the railways and the bombings, so it took a long time.
But--

Oh, yes, but not in the sense that we could get out, only in the sense that we were shunted to the side, you know, to-- so we were already in Czechoslovakia when we were shunted to the side somewhere.
It was quite near Theresienstadt.
And, of course, we were locked-- locked-- cattle trucks, naturally, as usual.
And suddenly, we heard a plane, like on our heads-- that near.
And the next thing was bombs.
We were bombed.
We were actually bombed.
And the-- the wagon, the cart next to us, had a direct hit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So the pressure made-- it must have been the pressure that made our door fly open, because the doors were not open, but the door became open.
Nothing happened in our carriage-- nothing-- not a shrapnel, nothing.
But as I said, a direct hit.
Now, in there were, among many, many others, two sisters, very close, like my sister and I-- very, very close.
And one of them had her head cut off by shrapnel.
And her sister [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was running next to the train, and her-- she was like a madwoman, screaming on the top of her voice, my sister, my sister.
And there was this body without a head.
I don't know what happened to her, whether she went crazy or not.
I wouldn't be surprised if she didn't.
This was at the end of the war.
They'd been together.
They've done it all together.
And then this girl is killed by a Russian plane.
Isn't that just so tragic?
And there was this poor girl, the sister, the one who was alive, running up and down, screaming, screaming, screaming.
But the other thing that happened there, somebody else was running up and down next to the train-- the woman who went blind.
She got her sight back.
The shock of the-- of the bombs, that sudden noise, or whatever it was, she got her sight back.
And she was also screaming, I can see!
I can-- she had her hands up.
I can see!
I can see!
I can see!
So that's-- at least, that's something.
Anyway, nothing happened.
As I said, nothing.
Quite a number of people died, of course, and a lot were-- were badly injured.
But anyway, such is life.
But nothing happened to us.
And I was talking to one of the Germans-- God knows why, but I was-- and asking where are they taking-- well, that's why, because I wanted to know it.
And he said, Theresienstadt.
[POINTING] That's where you go.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So, again, I didn't believe him, of course.
I never believed that I could possibly die.
We were taken into Theresienstadt and were taken into a particular part, which was somewhat better, in the sense that Theresienstadt, the stadt itself was-- had typhus.
And literally-- and I mean literally-- when you walked on the streets, you were walking in-- in effluence.
The stink there was something unbelievable.
But we were taken-- just not out-- the whole thing is that size-- but into a little compound where there was no typhus, and that point.
Neither was there any filth on the floor or on the ground or anywhere.
So what-- we were housed in one of these barracks, and it wasn't-- certainly wasn't too bad.
We didn't know what was going to happen to us.
We got there on the second of May.
And I can't say very much, other than I'm sure we didn't get anything to eat, because there, I do remember, by then, being awfully hungry.
But we really didn't see too many Germans.
We did see some Germans.
But we didn't see too many Germans.
And then, on the 5th of May, we didn't see any Germans.
But what we did see was Red Cross.
And we didn't want to believe it, of course.
But there is was-- no German.
And the Red Cross-- in fact, we saw a Red Cross van even a day before.
We saw a Red Cross van going past, which, of course, was incredibly big deal-- incredible, miracle.
There's a Red Cross van.

Well, what had happened was, of course-- it is now well known-- that the Red Cross took Theresienstadt over because the-- the commandant of Theresienstadt-- because the Jews of Europe, the surviving Jews, were being transported into Theresienstadt, which had a brand-new gas chamber.
And they were-- they were-- the soldier who was talking to me was quite right.
They were going to kill us all.
That was going to be it.
But the Red Cross made a deal with the commandant.
And he was-- he was offered his freedom, that can-- he will disappear.
They help him out of Theresienstadt.
He will disappear.
He won't be tried-- if he hands the Jews over.
I'm afraid I don't know.
I'm very sorry.
Don't know.
But that-- so that was-- that was-- that was what happened.
And he got away.
And I think his second in command, as well.
If I remember correctly, there were two people who were whisked away and disappeared and safe.
Because otherwise, they would have hung.
So we were actually taken over by the Red Cross.
And we-- it was very hard.
We didn't know all this, of course.
All we knew was that suddenly we were given-- I think I was given an apple or something or some milk.
I was given some milk.
And-- and-- but we still didn't feel free, of course.
We didn't-- I think-- well, we weren't free.
We were not free.
But there were no Germans.
So there was every hope then that this is really going to be the end.
And then, on the 8th of May, I-- I-- I couldn't sleep.
I don't know why I couldn't sleep.
But I couldn't sleep in the evening.
And my sister and my mother were asleep next to me.
And I was-- I wasn't asleep.
And pretty late -- of course, I don't know exactly what time, because we had no watches, but I would have thought around 12:00 or thereabouts-- I heard singing.
And you know what the singing was?
It was 'The Internationale.' Do you know, as I said that now, the-- the cold-- I-- cold shiver had just run down on me.
Can you tell me what 'The Internationale' is?
Oh, it's the-- the communist-- the communist hymn.
And I heard them sing that.
That's what they marched in with.
And so I knew that now we are free.
And I remember running out into-- in front of the barrack.
And there were a few other people who obviously were not asleep, and they also ran out.
I said, they're here.
The Russians are here.
And they were.
And, now, this is also a moment that will stay with me till the day I die.
And for this reason, I shall never hate the Russians, as is generally done.
Ah.
